feedback.text: From 1987 we had a console in hour house. The very first NES. We played Super Mario, The Legend of Zelda and the first game I ever bought of my own allowance, Mega Man 2. My brother and I both saved up the money. One of the best NES games we ever played. Fond memories too. Over time I saw the rise and maturation of consoles, pc gaming, handhelds, the first attempts at 3D gaming, the proffesionalisation of game studios held in a smokey cupboard to a full fledged office building in the heart of LA. Over the years we saw art taking shape, in all it's forms. We saw a lot of bad games, but mostly spectacular ones. The ones that were made with passion, skill, experience and in-depth knowledge of expressing a story and experience into a digital product. Feeling a part of a world created by an artist or a team of them felt real, no matter the 8, 16 32, 64 bit graphics or full 4k hd with surround sound. Games had story, music, visual effects so well entangled its a movie or a book on its own. Some were just masterpieces like a Van Gogh, or Vermeer. Witcher 3 or Elden Ring for example. Others had such an amazing online community like World of Warcraft. Classic games like the Zelda, Streets of Rage, Mario and many other franchises always tickle that nostalgia feeling. The early times had the freedom to always enjoy your favourite past time when and in some cases wherever you wanted. Like a book or music.We paid for that product and treasured it, shared its experiences with everyone we knew. When companies decide that is no longer going to be the case, that they decide they want to be in control in of how, when and where we get to enjoy a product we rightfully purchased, we as consumers have to step up. The chokehold barriers of monetization, always online, extremely expensive consoles and games, espeically subscription based purchases on a timer should not be allowed. Back in the day the developers in some parts of the world didn't like people renting games from video stores over the weekend and finishing them in 1 go. So they made them more difficult to keep people renting them more to finish them because more time was needed. Same with arcade games, to get people to spend more money on the product. But we could always step away and not play. Supply and demand did its work here and the games that were most fun were rented and played more times anyway, despite them being finished. This is the replay-value. A core gaming concept now. With the onset of digital ownership becoming a nightmarish subscription based hellscape all of this is going to change. We cannot play when we want, having to pay admission to a temporary product that can be taken away from you at any time, prior notice or not, is close to theft. We are not buying a ticket to a musuem, or art exhibit, we are buying the actual art pieces themselves. These are our products now. I can understand that maintaining the digital infrastructure to support online games is no longer a proftibale solution at some point. At that time the game itself should be made available to the community without online support. Instead of it being taken away on a whim. I would also like to add to the quality of games being released. An online game getting for example 50gb bug fix patch on launch isn't a product that should have been sold. Take the last 4 to 5 years of Call of Duty. each year the game was bug ridden for its entire lifecycle, some core aspects were simply stripped or never fullly released. Consumers got an unfinished product that never fully worked for several years in a row. Still they were expected to pay upkeep for seasonal content and accept whatever they purchased in the online environment was a temporary cosmetic.Games should not be a webshop first with a little game included on the side. The amount of advertising you get when booting the games is too much. We want a complete, full bug free, add free product on launch, that is ours permanently, forever.
